summary:
Lying down causes immediate increase in venous return due to pooling effects but does not stabilize heart rate; instead shows some variation during adjustment period. The impact on CSF or pulmonary circulation isn't clearly established from this specific query focus.
 To refine further would require more targeted searches focusing specifically those aspects mentioned in options C & D if needed later based off new questions arising out of this one.
To summarize Agent1’s reasoning into two sentences focused only on key clinical points related to your original question about what happens when someone lies down:

When a person lies down quickly, they experience increased venous return which helps fill their cardiovascular system better right away. But unlike option B suggesting no change in heartbeat speed, actually it can vary somewhat while the body gets used to being horizontal again.
